messages.content: What structures can be visualized after enlarging the sphenoidal ostium and introducing the endoscope into the sphenoid sinus?
It is possible to visualize the sellar floor, clivus, planum sphenoidalis, and the bony protuberances of the internal carotid artery and optic nerve, which form the opticocarotid recess.
What is visualized when the bone of the sellar floor is opened?
Opening the sellar floor allows visualization of the pituitary gland enveloped in its connective tissue, the diaphragma sellae, the anterior part of the circle of Willis, and the optic nerves.
What does opening the ethmoidosphenoidal planum allow access to?
Opening the ethmoidosphenoidal planum allows access to the anterior cranial fossa, with visualization of the basal frontal lobes and olfactory nerves.
What can be accessed by opening the lateral walls of the sphenoid sinus, and what can be visualized by opening the clivus?

Opening the lateral walls of the sphenoid sinus provides access to the cavernous sinus and its compartment, as well as the carotid siphon and the branches of the trigeminal nerve. Opening the clivus allows visualization of the basilar artery with its quadrifurcation and the third nerve.
What is the purpose of executing brain biopsies?
The purpose is to determine the diagnosis of intracranial lesions in order to assess the best treatment for the patient.
What are the two types of stereotactic biopsy mentioned?
The two types are 'frame-based' (using a stereotactic frame fixed to the skull with screws under local anesthesia) and 'frameless' (using neuronavigation systems).
Why is knowledge of three-dimensional syntopy of the brain important in stereotactic biopsy?
It is essential in order to reach the target while avoiding damage to important and/or vital structures such as white matter, nuclei, and vessels along the way.
How does an obstruction of the aqueduct of Sylvius affect the ventricles?

An obstruction of the aqueduct of Sylvius causes enlargement of the two lateral and the third ventricle because CSF produced in the choroid plexus of the supratentorial compartment cannot flow into the fourth ventricle.
What causes CSF accumulation in a lateral ventricle in non-communicating hydrocephalus?

In non-communicating hydrocephalus, no visible obstruction is apparent, and ventricle enlargement is due to normal CSF production and abnormal CSF reabsorption, such as after subarachnoid bleeding when hemoglobin degradation products obstruct the normal reabsorption pathway.
Why does ataxia of deambulation occur in normal pressure hydrocephalus?

The lateral expansion of the lateral ventricles compresses the pyramidal tract fibers, especially those for leg innervation located medially in the internal capsule, leading to ataxia of deambulation.
What is the first type of surgical operation used for treating hydrocephalus?
The first type is the insertion of a shunt, which diverts CSF from the ventricles to another cavity where it can be absorbed, with the most common being the ventriculoperitoneal shunt.
How is third ventriculocisternostomy performed?

A single burr hole is made in the frontal bone, an endoscope is inserted through the brain parenchyma of the frontal lobe to reach the lateral ventricle, then into the foramen of Monro to visualize third ventricle structures. The floor of the third ventricle is surgically perforated near the mammillary bodies to allow CSF to flow into the interpeduncular and prepontine cisterns.
What anatomical landmarks are visualized by the endoscope in the lateral ventricle during third ventriculocisternostomy?
The landmarks include the foramen of Monro, choroid plexus, thalamostriate vein, choroidal vein, vein of the septum pellucidum, columns of the fornix, and head of the caudate nucleus.
What is the purpose of the opening created in the floor of the third ventricle during third ventriculocisternostomy?
The opening allows CSF from the lateral ventricles to flow directly into the interpeduncular and prepontine cisterns, bypassing the obstruction.
Who is the author of 'Principles of Neural Science' and what is notable about him?
The author is Eric Kandel, who won the Nobel Prize in 2000. He was born in Vienna but is an American naturalized citizen.
Which book is described as 'the most detailed anatomical atlas'?
Pernkopf's 'Atlas of Topographical and Applied Human Anatomy. Vol. 1: Head and Neck' is described as the most detailed anatomical atlas.
What is the recommended book for understanding brain stem and cerebellum from histologic, anatomic, and radiologic points of view?
'Duvernoy's Atlas of the Human Brain Stem and Cerebellum' by Naidich PT et al., published by Springer Wien New York in 2008, is recommended for this purpose.
Which book provides an endoscopic perspective on brain anatomy?
'Endoscopic Anatomy for Neurosurgery' by Perneczky A, Tschabitscher M, and Resch KDM, published by Georg Thieme Verlag in 1993, provides an endoscopic perspective.
Which book is described as 'the most didactic collection of images and schemas' for neuroanatomy?
Netter's 'The CIBA Collection of Medical Illustrations. Vol. 1. Nervous System. Part 1: Anatomy and Physiology' is described as the most didactic collection of images and schemas.
What specific chapters are recommended for reading in Niewenhuys et al.'s 'The Human Central Nervous System'?
The section on the neocortex (chapter 15 of the fourth edition) and the limbic system (chapter 23) are highly recommended.
Which book is considered a milestone for becoming a neurosurgeon, and which volumes are specifically recommended?

Yasargil's 'Microneurosurgery' is considered a milestone. Vol. I is recommended for detailed description of the anatomy of the cisterns and arteries of the brain, and Vol. IVA for topographic anatomy of the brain and description of some operative approaches.
Which German-language book is an extensive summary of neuroanatomy written by professors from the Medical University of Vienna?
'Neuroanatomie' by Firbas W, Gruber H, and Mayr R, published by Verlag Wilhelm Maudric in 1995, is an extensive summary of neuroanatomy in German.
What techniques are used for dissecting human cadavers mentioned in the content?
The techniques include the Klingler technique for the dissection of the white fiber system, plastination, casts, and sections of fixed specimens.
How are blood vessels colored in some anatomical specimens?
The colour of the vessels is due to the injection of blue silicon in the venous system and red silicon in the arterial system.
What are the main topics covered on page 59 of the Brain Anatomy Booklet?

Page 59 covers the skull, including bones of the skull base, foramina of the skull base, 3D CT of the skull base, cranial nerves I and II, and the right parasellar compartment (cavernous sinus) with related cranial nerves.
What anatomical structures are shown in the axial section of the brain from the document?
The axial section of the brain shows bilateral herniation of the uncus in the tentorial incisura and compression of the cerebral crus.
What does the latero-medial dissection of the right hemisphere show?
The latero-medial dissection of the right hemisphere shows the relationship between the cortex, white fiber system, insula and limbic system (M1-M2: bifurcation of the arteria cerebri media).
What is listed under 'Basal surface'?
Under 'Basal surface', the items listed are 'Orbital frontal lobes' and 'Basal surface of the brain, with injected arteries and veins'.
What are the main topics covered in the Brain Stem and Cerebellum section of the document?
The section covers the sintopy of the brain stem (dorsal view), the dentate nucleus and cerebellar peduncles, and brain stem dorsal views.
What specific anatomical connections or syntopies are listed in the content for the cerebellum?
The content lists the syntopy of the left dentate nucleus and the connection of the nucleus dentatus with the red nucleus.
What does MR Venography in a sagittal view identify in a young woman under contraceptive therapy?
MR Venography in a sagittal view identifies complete thrombosis of the right sinus transversus (indicated by arrows) in a young woman under contraceptive therapy.
What is the purpose of 'hanging' the dura mater to the borders of the bone during a neurosurgical approach?
The 'hanging' of the dura mater to the borders of the bone is performed to avoid that an epidural hematoma, after the operation, can cause compression of the underlying brain.
For what specific conditions is a frontotemporosphenoidoparietal craniotomy generally used?
This kind of large exposure is generally used for the operation of decompressive craniotomy, used in some specific conditions to decompress the brain following bleeding or head trauma.
What does endonasal transillumination make it possible to visualize?
Endonasal transillumination makes it possible to visualize the areas of the skull base which are reachable by means of the endoscopic transsphenoidal approach.
How is the sphenoid sinus oriented to assist anatomical orientation in the endoscopic transsphenoidal approach?
The sphenoid sinus is 'encircled' in a clock, with the hypophysis in the center, clivus at h. 6, internal carotid arteries at h. 3 and 9, and optic nerves and optico-carotid recess at h. 2 and 10.
What artery is noted as duplicated on the right side in the description of the extended endoscopic approach?
The duplicated superior cerebellar artery on the right side is noted in the extended endoscopic approach (P1: posterior cerebral artery, pars precommunicalis; P2: pars postcommunicalis).
What type of glioma is biopsied via stereotactic biopsy in the given content, and how is it defined?
A 'butterfly' glioma is biopsied via stereotactic biopsy, so defined because it expands between the two frontal lobes via the corpus callosum.
What is the site of opening to perform a cisternostomy in the third ventricle?
The site of the opening is the floor of the third ventricle, to perform a cisternostomy (communication between the 3° ventricle and the pontine cistern).
